And there's it's not like they they have to speak to their wife, because they're not certain enough to make that decision on their own behalf, because if they turn out to be wrong, they don't want to get a slap when they walk in the door, right? Now, because of that, I'm keenly aware that there has to be what's called a certainty gradient. I didn't really talk about this before. I used to talk about back in the day, I call it a certainty gradient. Meaning, you guys know I was a science man. I'm actually a fucking big giant nerd, believe it or not. When I go on an airplane and travel, I always buy science magazines. I was going to be a doctor. I love science, right? So I'm really into physics and both on relativity and also quantum mechanics. And now what typically happens is they will engage in what's called objection hopping. If their first objection was let me think about it, they'll now say, oh, no, yo, wow, it sounds really good. Let me just, can I call you back in an hour? And this is when the manual starts to matter, because it's in that moment when they say, let me call you back. He's got to like do the, it becomes very obvious. See, the first time they hit you with an objection and you deflect it, totally fine. Makes total sense. Nothing wrong or weird about it. But please, don't deflect more than once. You're done. You deflect the second time. That's it. You're broken rapport. They no longer trust you. You might as well end the conversation. You cannot deflect the second objection. You have to answer it head on. Okay. And so what I would do back in the day and just, you know, I mean, listen, in the average sale, you might get hit with four different objections. You do just as the way people are. They hop from one objection to the next, right? So every morning and every afternoon, I give these meetings and I go through objection. Got to get you new markers. Okay, cool. All right. The first question is from Dana Yval. Can someone suggest to me how I can find a great online mentor? How to find a great online mentor? Dana, you know, the more I'm your mentor, I mean, the reason I'm in your ear every day is to mentor you, to coach you, to make sure you stay on track and don't get negative. You don't just hope. That's why these daily motivations are so crucial, right? It's not, it's, you know, again, you know, it's a pain in the ass for me. Come on. I don't, you think I really, every single fucking day I want to get out there and do one? I don't, but I do it because I know it matters to people. And when I see the comments, this is why I love getting the comments on the member sites because it reminds me that this is a great thing I'm doing. So I'm your mentor. Number one, you know, when I was in jail, I used Tom Wolf as my mentor. I read Bonfire the Values. I dissected his book, you know, Bonfire, line by line. I taught myself to write by reading his book. He was my mentor. He didn't even know it. He never met me before. I'm sure he's read about it since then, right? But at the time, he didn't know who I was, right? You don't have to know your mentors to use them as mentors. That being said, okay, you know, there's many different types of mentors. What are you looking for, Dana? Are you looking for someone who's going to be an accountability mentor to you? Someone who's going to keep you on track? That's accountability coaching, you know? And I thought of offering that, you know, to people and, you know, training some certain people to be those coaches. I haven't done it because, you know, it's not a money-making thing. And I have to be able to organize it logistically. But there are companies that do that. They're accountability coaches. Just be really, really careful, okay? Because most of them are scams. They just are. They're just there. It's not like this, Dana. It's just not like this, okay? Where you guys all know that everything I do is not designed to separate you from your money. It's just not, okay? Everything I do, I over-deliver in value 10 times, all right? Of course, I'm entitled to make a lot of money doing this, but I make sure that whatever I do, it's like not even a close exchange. I give you, you know, $10,000 of value as you with $10 in return. Seriously, that's what it's like, right? It's very rare in this world because what will happen with most mentors is they'll start you off with a mentorship. It seems like a great deal. And before you know it, they're upselling you into fucking everything else. And then you're fucking like, you're like, what the fuck happened? You know? It's terrible. And you know, it's not pure. Nothing wrong with upselling. Sometimes I'll offer you stuff. But I know when I offer things, it's always like a, it's a massively ridiculously great deal. But it's just mostly not like that around the world. Be careful. So how do you choose a mentor? Right? It's a good question, right? Well, there's three things that you want in a mentor. Okay? Number one, all right, is that a mentor has to, a good, a true mentor that really can help you, has to have done what you want to do yourself at the highest level. They have to have been in the real world and accomplished what you want to accomplish and been there and not read books, not written books or talked on stage about all these jokers out there and never fucking sold anything or really the only way they were, they made money, you know, outselling fucking programs to people, business opportunities. That's the biggest scam. It drives me crazy, but it's very prevalent. So number one, make sure that whoever your mentor is, that person has done, has in the real world, I mean, they built the real business doing what you want to do. That's number one. Number two is that some of the greatest players in the world make the worst coaches. Just because someone's a great player, a great businessman, a big success, they might not be able to coach or mentor their way out of a paper fucking bag. They have to be terrible mentors. Good question. Very good question. This is what when you cross over from the level of being consciously competent to being unconsciously competent. In other words, when you are consciously competent, I'd be lying to you if I said that you can actually take in, unless you're a natural bone closer, right? That you could take in all the information you've heard and tailor your patterns on the fly that you've written to perfectly match it and keep the whole thing all the way. This is very, very rare. People can do that. I can do it, and some people can, but very, very few, right? In the beginning, okay, what you want to do is what you want to do is to make sure that after you've done a bunch of presentations, you'll know what your objections are. I want you to see those answers into your language patterns. The best time to answer your objections before it comes up is possible. You can anticipate it, but if you know what the most five objections are, then I want you to put them in those loops. Make sure they're all in there. Once you start doing this more and more, and you get really unconsciously competent, you will naturally be able to tailor these language patterns to, you'll be able to move around. Trust me, do this.
